title: Exception Handling
description: Dealing with runtime errors on purpose: throw, try, and catch — and the crucial distinction from the undefined behavior we've seen elsewhere.
objectives: Understand what an exception is and when to throw one
Use try/catch to handle an exception gracefully
Distinguish exceptions from the undefined behavior seen in Arrays and Pointers

sections.text: 1. The Problem: Errors That Happen While Running
Some problems can't be caught by the compiler ahead of time — dividing by a value that turns out to be 0 only becomes a problem once the program is actually running with that specific input.
An exception is a signal, raised with throw, that an error occurred — interrupting normal execution and looking for code prepared to handle that specific problem.
runtime_error is a standard exception type from <stdexcept>, carrying a descriptive message. throw immediately stops divide() from continuing normally and starts searching for a handler.

sections.title: The pieces
points: try { ... } — code that might throw an exception
catch (Type &e) { ... } — runs only if a matching exception was thrown inside the try block
e.what() — returns the descriptive message the exception was created with

sections.output:
Error: Division by zero
Program continues normally.

sections.text: Notice cout << divide(10, 0); never actually prints a result — the moment throw executes inside divide(), control jumps straight to the matching catch block, skipping the rest of the try block entirely. Crucially, the program doesn't crash: it recovers and "Program continues normally." still prints.

Multiple catch blocks can follow one try, each handling a different exception type — checked in order, similar in spirit to else if from Conditional Statements. exception is the general base type most standard exceptions derive from, useful as a catch-all.

This is an important distinction: an out-of-bounds array access (from Arrays) or dereferencing a dangling pointer (from delete) is undefined behavior, not a thrown exception — nothing throws, nothing can be caught, and the program's behavior is simply unreliable. C++'s built-in array and pointer operations do not automatically protect you with exceptions the way, say, calling .at() on some STL containers later will. Exceptions are for errors a program explicitly detects and reports with throw, not a safety net that catches every possible mistake automatically.

sections.title: A fire alarm and an evacuation plan
sections.text: throw is like pulling a fire alarm — a clear signal that something specific has gone wrong. try/catch is the building's evacuation plan: normal activity (the try block) is interrupted, everyone follows the prepared response (the catch block) for that specific alarm, and life goes on afterward instead of the whole building collapsing.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming every runtime problem automatically becomes a catchable exception — undefined behavior (like out-of-bounds array access) is not an exception and cannot be caught.
Forgetting #include <stdexcept> when using standard exception types like runtime_error.

Catching by value instead of by reference — catch (runtime_error &e) is generally preferred over catch (runtime_error e), for reasons that become clearer once inheritance-based exception hierarchies are involved.
Wrapping code in try/catch defensively everywhere, even where nothing can realistically throw — adds noise without benefit.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does the throw keyword do?
questions.answer: It raises an exception, immediately interrupting normal execution and transferring control to a matching catch block, if one exists.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a try/catch block?
questions.answer: try marks code that might throw an exception; catch defines a handler that runs if a matching exception is thrown inside that try block, allowing the program to recover instead of crashing.

questions.question: Can multiple catch blocks follow a single try block?
questions.answer: Yes — each catch block can handle a different exception type, checked in order, similar to an if/else if chain.

questions.question: Is an out-of-bounds array access an exception?
questions.answer: No — it's undefined behavior. Nothing is thrown and nothing can be caught; this is a key distinction from an explicitly thrown exception.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Exception handling deals with runtime errors on purpose: throw raises an exception, such as throw runtime_error("Division by zero");, immediately interrupting normal execution. A try block wraps code that might throw, and a matching catch block handles that specific exception type, letting the program recover and continue instead of crashing — multiple catch blocks can handle different exception types in order. It's important to distinguish this from undefined behavior, like an out-of-bounds array access: that's not an exception, nothing is thrown, and try/catch cannot catch it.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Exception Handling
sections.text: throw = pull the fire alarm. try = the area that might have a fire. catch = the evacuation plan for that specific alarm. Undefined behavior ≠ exception — no alarm rings at all.

codeExamples.output:
Error: Division by zero
Program continues normally.
